title: The Best Practices for Protecting Your Energy
meta_description: Discover simple yet powerful practices to protect your energy and stop absorbing others' emotions. No fluff, just real guidance for sensitive souls.
og_title: The Best Practices for Protecting Your Energy
og_description: Discover simple yet powerful practices to protect your energy and stop absorbing others' emotions. No fluff, just real guidance for sensitive souls.
body:
You're Not Broken - You're Just Wide Open

Let me guess. You walk into a room and suddenly feel heavy. You sit next to someone at a coffee shop and their anxiety lands in your chest like a stone. You watch the news for ten minutes and feel like you've been dragged through a war zone. And somewhere along the way, someone told you you're too sensitive. Too much. That you need to toughen up.

Here's the thing they didn't tell you: you're not broken. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. You're picking up on things that others miss - energy, emotion, the unspoken stuff that hangs in the air like humidity before a storm. That's not a flaw. That's a gift. But gifts need boundaries, right?! If you don't know how to protect your energy, that gift turns into a weight that drags you down.

I'm Kalesh. I've been where you are. I've spent years learning how to hold my sensitivity without letting it drown me. And I'm going to show you the practices that actually work - not the fluffy, feel-good stuff that leaves you still exhausted. The real, gritty, ground-level tools that let you walk through the world without absorbing everyone else's garbage.

Does that land? Good. Let's get into it.

What "Protecting Your Energy" Actually Means

First, let's clear something up. Protecting your energy isn't about building walls or shutting people out. It's not about becoming cold or distant. It's about learning to filter what comes in so you can stay open without getting wrecked.

Think of it like this: you don't walk around with your front door wide open all day, letting anyone wander in and sit on your couch. You have a door. You have a lock. You decide who comes in and when. Your energy works the same way. When you're wide open all the time, you're letting every stray emotion, every anxious thought, every piece of heavy energy from the people around you take up space in your living room. No wonder you're exhausted.

Protecting your energy isn't about becoming less sensitive - it's about becoming smart enough to know what's yours and what isn't.

That's the core insight right there. Most of what you feel throughout the day? It's not yours. It's the guy on the subway who's late for work. It's your coworker who's fighting with their partner. It's the collective tension of a city full of people running on empty. And if you don't learn to tell the difference, you'll spend your whole life trying to fix things that were never your problem to begin with.

Practice #1: The Morning Filter - Set Your Intention Before You Step Out

If you're anything like I was, you wake up and immediately start absorbing. You check your phone. You feel the weight of the day. You get hit with your partner's mood or your kid's stress before you've even had coffee. That's a recipe for starting your day already drained.

Here's what works instead. Before you get out of bed, take three breaths. Just three. Put your hand on your chest and say out loud or silently: "I am only responsible for my own energy today. What's not mine will pass through without sticking."

That's it. That's the whole practice. It takes thirty seconds, but it sets a filter for the day. You're telling your nervous system, "Hey, we're not collecting everyone's junk today." And your nervous system listens because you've given it a clear instruction.

You can add a visual if that helps. Imagine a bubble of light around you - not a hard shell, but a soft, permeable layer that lets good stuff in and lets the heavy stuff slide off. Like a raincoat for your soul. Sounds a little woo-woo, I know. But try it for a week and see if you don't feel a difference.

If you want to go deeper with this kind of practice, there's a great book called The Psychic Workbook that walks you through exactly how to build these filters. See on Amazon (paid link)

Practice #2: The Grounding Cord - Your Escape Hatch for Overload

You know that moment when you're in a conversation and suddenly you're flooded with someone else's anger, sadness, or fear? It hits you in the gut. Your chest tightens. Your mind starts racing. You want to get out, but you're stuck.

That's when you need a grounding cord. It's the single most effective tool I've found for instantly releasing energy that isn't yours.

Here's how it works. Sit or stand comfortably. Close your eyes if you can. Imagine a cord extending from the base of your spine - not your head, your spine - down through the floor, through the ground, all the way to the center of the earth. See it as a thick, sturdy rope, or a root, or a beam of light. Whatever feels right to you.

Now, imagine all the energy that's not yours - the anxiety, the heaviness, the noise - draining down that cord. Let it flow out of you like water running out of a sink. Don't try to control it. Just let it go. The earth can handle it. The earth is huge. It's been processing energy for billions of years. Your coworker's stress is nothing to it.

Stay with that for a minute or two. You'll feel your body settle. Your breath will deepen. You'll remember that you have a foundation that nothing can shake.

I do this in the middle of crowded rooms. I do it before meetings. I do it when I'm lying in bed and can't sleep because I'm still carrying the day. It works every single time. Not because it's magic, but because it's a physical anchor for your nervous system. You're telling your body, "I'm safe. I'm here. I'm not floating away."

Practice #3: The Energy Shower - Washing Off the Day

You wouldn't go to bed without washing the dirt off your body after a long day, would you? So why would you go to bed without washing the energy off your field?

I call this the energy shower. You can do it in the actual shower if you want, or you can do it standing in your room before bed. Either way, the idea is the same: you're consciously rinsing off everything you picked up during the day.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Take a few deep breaths. Now, imagine a stream of warm, golden light coming down from above - like a waterfall of pure, clean energy. Let it pour over your head, your shoulders, your chest, your arms, your legs. See it washing away any sticky, heavy, or dull energy that's clinging to you. It flows down your body and into the ground, taking everything with it.

You can add water if you're in the shower. Let the actual water be a physical symbol of what you're doing energetically. As the water runs over you, say: "I release what is not mine. I return to myself."

This practice is especially important if you work with people, if you're a parent, if you're in a helping profession, or if you just live in a city. You're picking up energy all day long. You need a way to clear it out. Otherwise, it accumulates. And accumulated energy turns into fatigue, brain fog, and that low-grade feeling of being "off" that you can't quite name.

If you're new to this kind of energetic hygiene, I highly recommend Radiant Mind by Judith Pennington. It's a practical guide that covers exactly these kinds of practices. See on Amazon (paid link)

Practice #4: The "Is This Mine?" Check-In

Here's a question that will change your life: when you feel a sudden shift in your mood, stop and ask yourself, "Is this mine?"

Seriously. Do it right now. Think about the last time you felt anxious, angry, or sad for no apparent reason. Was it really yours? Or did you walk into a room and pick it up from someone else?

Most of the time, it's not yours. Your energy is like a tuning fork - it vibrates at your own frequency until you get near someone else's frequency, and then you start vibrating in harmony with them. That's empathy. It's a beautiful thing, but it's also a trap if you don't know how to unhook.

So here's the practice. Anytime you feel a strong emotion that doesn't seem to have a clear cause in your own life, pause. Take a breath. Ask: "Is this mine?"

If the answer is no - and it often will be - then you have a choice. You can let it go. You can imagine it floating away like a cloud. You can send it back to its owner with love (or not, depending on the situation). You don't have to carry it. You never had to carry it.

This simple check-in is the foundation of all energy protection. Without it, you're just guessing. With it, you start to build awareness. And awareness is the key to everything.

Practice #5: Boundaries That Aren't Walls

I said earlier that protecting your energy isn't about building walls. But it is about having boundaries. And there's a difference.

Walls keep everything out - the good and the bad. Boundaries let you choose what comes in. Think of it like a fence with a gate. You get to decide who opens the gate and when.

For sensitive people, boundaries often feel hard because we don't want to hurt anyone's feelings. We don't want to be rude. We don't want to seem cold. So we let people dump their stuff on us, and then we resent them for it.

Here's the truth: you can be kind and have boundaries. In fact, you can be kinder when you have boundaries, because you're not running on empty. You have energy left to actually show up when it matters.

So try this. The next time someone wants to vent to you for the tenth time about the same problem, say this: "I can't hold this right now, but I love that you trust me." Or "I have to take care of myself today, but I'm here for you tomorrow."

That's it. That's a boundary. It's honest. It's compassionate. And it protects your energy without burning a bridge.

You might feel guilty the first few times. That's normal. Guilt is just your old programming trying to keep you small. Do it anyway. Your nervous system will thank you.

Practice #6: The Daily Energy Audit

when it comes down to it, take five minutes to do an energy audit. This is not a journaling exercise about your feelings. It's a practical check-in on what you're carrying.

Ask yourself: "What energy did I take on today that wasn't mine?"

List it in your head or on paper. The tension from the meeting. Your friend's breakup story. The stress of the news. The weird vibe from the stranger on the bus.

Then, one by one, imagine releasing each one. You can blow it out like a candle. You can hand it back to the universe. You can drop it into the grounding cord. Whatever works.

The point is to consciously clear your field before you sleep. If you go to bed still carrying other people's energy, you'll dream about their stuff instead of processing your own. You'll wake up tired. You'll start the next day already behind.

This practice takes five minutes. It saves you hours of low-grade exhaustion. Do it every night for a week and see what happens.

Practice #7: The Power of No-Tech Zones

Here's something nobody tells you: your phone is an energy vampire. Not because it's evil, but because it's a constant stream of other people's energy. Every notification is someone wanting something from you. Every scroll through social media is a flood of emotions, opinions, and dramas that have nothing to do with you.

If you're sensitive, you're especially vulnerable to this. You're not just seeing words and pictures - you're feeling the energy behind them. That angry comment thread? You feel the anger. That sad post from a friend? You feel the sadness. That selected highlight reel? You feel the insecurity.

So here's the practice: create no-tech zones in your day. At least one hour before bed, put the phone away. No screens. No notifications. No input. Just you, the quiet, and your own energy.

You can read a physical book. You can take a walk. You can sit and breathe. The goal is to give your nervous system a break from processing other people's frequencies.

If you want a good read for those no-tech hours, check out Radiant Mind - it's full of practices exactly like these. See on Amazon (paid link)

Practice #8: The Energy Shield - Not a Wall, a Filter

I've avoided talking about shields until now because a lot of people teach them wrong. They teach you to build a thick, impenetrable barrier around yourself. And that works for about five minutes, until you realize you've cut yourself off from everything - including joy, connection, and love.

Instead, think of your energy shield as a filter. It lets in what's nourishing and keeps out what's not. It's not a wall. It's a smart door.

Here's how to set one up. Close your eyes. Imagine a sphere of light around you - about arm's length in every direction. See it as a soft, glowing membrane. Now, give it an intention. Say: "This filter allows in only what serves my highest good. Everything else passes through without effect."

That's it. You don't have to visualize it perfectly. You don't have to feel anything. You just set the intention, and your energy field will follow.

You can reset this shield anytime you feel it's getting clogged. I do it when I walk into a crowded space, before a difficult conversation, or anytime I feel like I'm starting to absorb again.

Practice #9: The "I'm Full" Pause

You know that moment when you're in a conversation and suddenly you feel like you can't breathe? Your chest gets tight. Your thoughts get fuzzy. You want to escape but you're stuck in politeness.

That's your nervous system saying, "I'm full." And in that moment, the best thing you can do is pause.

You don't have to explain. You don't have to apologize. Just say, "I need a moment." Or "Excuse me, I need to step out for a second." Then go to the bathroom, or step outside, or just turn away and take three deep breaths.

In that pause, run the grounding cord. Do the "is this mine?" check. Let the excess energy drain. Then come back when you're ready.

This practice is revolutionary because it gives you permission to prioritize your own energy in real time. Most of us were taught to push through. To be polite. To stay until the end. But pushing through when you're full only leads to burnout. The pause is a reset button. Use it.

Practice #10: The Nightly Reset - Full Body Release

Before you go to sleep tonight, do this. Lie on your back with your arms at your sides. Take a deep breath. Starting at your feet, tense every muscle in your body as tight as you can. Hold for a count of five. Then release completely.

Move up to your calves, tense and release. Your thighs. Your hips. Your stomach. Your chest. Your hands. Your arms. Your shoulders. Your face. Tense everything, then let everything go.

As you release, imagine all the energy you've collected during the day flowing out of your body and into the bed. See it draining away like sand through a sieve. You don't need it. You never needed it.

This practice combines physical release with energetic release. It's incredibly effective for people who carry tension in their bodies - and if you're sensitive, you probably do. You're holding other people's stress in your shoulders, your jaw, your lower back. This practice lets it go.

After you've done the full body release, lie still for a few minutes. Let your body feel the difference between holding and letting go. That's the state you want to sleep in.

You're Not Alone in This

Look, I know this is a lot. Ten practices. But you don't have to do all of them at once. Pick one. Just one. Try it for a week. See how it feels. Then add another.

The point isn't to be perfect at protecting your energy. The point is to build awareness. Every time you check in, every time you ask "is this mine?", every time you run that grounding cord, you're strengthening your ability to be in the world without being drained by it.

You're not broken. You're not too sensitive. You're just learning how to handle a gift that most people don't even know exists. And that takes practice. That takes patience. That takes a lot of nights where you feel like you're failing at it.

But you're not failing. You're learning. And every
